subject: Mathematics

unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 3! In earlier units you learned to add and subtract smaller numbers. Now you will grow those skills so you can work with numbers up to three digits – that means numbers all the way up to 999. You will also learn how to handle money, adding and subtracting amounts in shillings so you can shop, save and give change like a real trader at the market.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, activities and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Primary Mathematics 0096 — Stage 3 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: This unit is built for self-paced learning. That means you move at your own speed. Read each explanation slowly, copy every worked example onto paper and try it yourself, and only move on when you feel ready. Your AI tutor is here day and night to give you extra questions, explain a step a different way, or mark your work. Your teacher will also meet with you about twice a week to help with anything that feels tricky.

Keep a pencil and paper beside you the whole time. Mathematics is not something you can learn only by reading – you must do it. Line up your digits neatly, work from right to left, and say each step out loud. When you get something wrong, that is not failure; it is a signpost showing you exactly what to practise next. Let us begin.
Add a 3-digit number to a single-digit, 2-digit or another 3-digit number.
Subtract a single-digit, 2-digit or 3-digit number from a 3-digit number.
Regroup correctly when carrying in addition and borrowing in subtraction.
Subtract across zeros (for example 400 − 256) without getting stuck.
Estimate sums and differences by rounding to the nearest 10 or 100.
Write amounts of money in decimal notation (shillings and cents).
Add and subtract money amounts and work out the correct change.
Check your answers using estimation and the inverse operation.

concepts.title: Place Value in 3-Digit Numbers
concepts.explanation: Before we add and subtract big numbers, we must be sure about place value. Every 3-digit number is built from hundreds, tens and ones. The digit on the far right is the ones, the middle digit is the tens, and the left digit is the hundreds. Let us look closely at the number 356:
concepts.example: The 3 is in the hundreds place, so it stands for 3 hundreds = 300.

concepts.title: Adding 3-Digit Numbers with Regrouping
concepts.explanation: Regrouping (also called carrying) happens whenever the digits in one column add up to 10 or more. When that happens, we cannot fit two digits into one column, so we write the ones digit of the total in that column and carry the ten to the next column on the left. Let us build this up gently, in three steps of difficulty. Adding a 3-digit number and a single digit
concepts.example: Example: 327 + 5. Line them up with the 5 under the ones digit 7.

concepts.title: Subtracting 3-Digit Numbers with Regrouping
concepts.explanation: Subtraction uses the same neat, right-to-left method, but instead of carrying we sometimes need to borrow (also called regrouping). We borrow whenever the top digit in a column is smaller than the digit we are taking away. We take one from the next column on the left and add it as ten to the column we are working on. Subtracting a single digit from a 3-digit number
concepts.example: Ones: 5 − 7 will not work, because 5 is smaller than 7. Borrow 1 ten from the tens place. The 2 tens become 1 ten, and the ones become 15. Now 15 − 7 = 8.

concepts.title: Subtracting Across Zeros
concepts.explanation: One kind of subtraction trips up many learners: subtracting when there are zeros in the top number, such as 400 − 256 or 700 − 24. There is nothing to borrow from a zero, so you have to borrow from further along. Let us go slowly. Ones: 0 − 6 will not work, and the tens digit is also 0, so we cannot borrow from it directly. We travel left to the hundreds. Take 1 hundred: the 4 hundreds become 3 hundreds, and the tens become 10.
concepts.example: Now borrow 1 of those tens for the ones: the tens become 9, and the ones become 10. Now 10 − 6 = 4.

concepts.title: Estimating Sums and Differences
concepts.explanation: An estimate is a quick, rough answer you work out before doing the full calculation. It is not a wild guess – it is a sensible number found by rounding. Estimating first is a habit that good mathematicians keep for life, because it warns you when a final answer has gone badly wrong. Example: estimate 328 + 156. Round 328 to 330 and 156 to 160. Then 330 + 160 = 490. The exact answer is 484, which is very close to 490, so we can trust it.
concepts.example: Example: estimate 583 − 247. Round 583 to 580 and 247 to 250. Then 580 − 250 = 330. The exact answer is 336, again very close. If Farah had calculated and got 536, the big gap from 330 would tell her to stop and check,…

concepts.title: Understanding Money Notation
concepts.explanation: Now we bring these skills to money. In this unit we work in shillings, and we use a decimal point to separate whole shillings from cents. There are 100 cents in one shilling, just as there are 100 ones in a group of hundreds – so money is really just place value wearing new clothes. 4 shillings and 24 cents is written Sh 4.24.
concepts.example: 10 shillings exactly is written Sh 10.00 (or simply Sh 10).

methods.title: How to add two 3-digit numbers
steps: Line up the numbers in columns and estimate the answer first.
Add the ones. If the total is 10 or more, write the ones digit and carry the ten.
Add the tens (plus any carry). Carry again if needed.
Add the hundreds (plus any carry). Compare with your estimate.

methods.title: How to subtract a 3-digit number
steps: Line up the numbers in columns and estimate the answer first.
Subtract the ones. If the top is smaller, borrow one ten first.
Subtract the tens, borrowing a hundred if needed.
Subtract the hundreds. Check by adding your answer back to the smaller number.

methods.title: How to calculate change
steps: Write the amount paid above the cost, lining up the decimal points.
Subtract the cost from the amount paid, borrowing across the decimal if needed.
Or count up from the cost to the amount paid – both give the same change.

methods.title: Subtract across a zero
steps: Solution: Borrow across the zeros.
800 becomes 7 hundreds, 9 tens and 10 ones.

methods.title: Estimate then calculate
steps: Solution: Estimate: 490 + 330 = 820.
Exact: ones 9 + 5 = 14, write 4 carry 1; tens 8 + 2 + 1 = 11, write 1 carry 1; hundreds 4 + 3 + 1 = 8.
The answer is 814, close to the estimate of 820.
It checks out.

methods.title: Add money with regrouped cents
steps: Solution: Cents: 45 + 75 = 120 = 1 shilling and 20 cents, write 20 carry 1.
Shillings: 8 + 6 + 1 = 15.
The answer is Sh 15.20.

workedExamples.title: Add a 3-digit and a 2-digit number
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Ones: 3 + 9 = 12, write 2, carry 1. Tens: 8 + 1 + 1 (carried) = 10, write 0, carry 1. Hundreds: 5 + 1 (carried) = 6. The answer is 602. Two carries in one sum – handled calmly, one column at a time.

workedExamples.title: Add two 3-digit numbers with a big total
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Ones: 5 + 5 = 10, write 0, carry 1. Tens: 2 + 7 + 1 = 10, write 0, carry 1. Hundreds: 7 + 2 + 1 = 10, write 0, carry 1 into a new thousands place. The answer is 1,000.

workedExamples.title: Subtract with two borrows
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Ones: 2 − 7 needs a borrow → 12 − 7 = 5 (tens drop from 3 to 2). Tens: 2 − 5 needs a borrow → 12 − 5 = 7 (hundreds drop from 6 to 5). Hundreds: 5 − 4 = 1. The answer is 175. Check: 175 + 457 = 632. Correct!

workedExamples.title: Subtract across a zero
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Borrow across the zeros. 800 becomes 7 hundreds, 9 tens and 10 ones. Ones: 10 − 6 = 4. Tens: 9 − 5 = 4. Hundreds: 7 − 3 = 4. The answer is 444.

workedExamples.title: Estimate then calculate
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Estimate: 490 + 330 = 820. Exact: ones 9 + 5 = 14, write 4 carry 1; tens 8 + 2 + 1 = 11, write 1 carry 1; hundreds 4 + 3 + 1 = 8. The answer is 814, close to the estimate of 820. It checks out.

workedExamples.title: Add money with regrouped cents
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Cents: 45 + 75 = 120 = 1 shilling and 20 cents, write 20 carry 1. Shillings: 8 + 6 + 1 = 15. The answer is Sh 15.20.

workedExamples.title: Subtract money
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Cents: 00 − 45 needs a borrow → 100 − 45 = 55. Shillings: after the borrow, 49 − 23 = 26. The answer is Sh 26.55.

workedExamples.title: A two-step money problem
workedExamples.prompt: Sofia has Sh 25.00. She buys a hat for Sh 8.50 and gloves for Sh 6.75. How much money does she have left?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: First add the costs: Sh 8.50 + Sh 6.75 = Sh 15.25. Then subtract from what she had: Sh 25.00 − Sh 15.25 = Sh 9.75. Two steps – add, then subtract – done in order.

workedExamples.title: Add money
workedExamples.prompt: Add money: a) Sh 3.25 + Sh 1.50 b) Sh 6.75 + Sh 2.35 c) Sh 5.99 + Sh 4.99.

workedExamples.title: Subtract money
workedExamples.prompt: Subtract money: a) Sh 10.00 − Sh 3.50 b) Sh 20.00 − Sh 7.25 c) Sh 5.75 − Sh 2.80.

workedExamples.title: Subtract across zeros
workedExamples.prompt: Subtract across zeros: a) 300 − 5 b) 700 − 24 c) 800 − 356 d) 400 − 256.

workedExamples.title: Practice 12
workedExamples.prompt: Add these larger sums, watching for a carry into the thousands: a) 725 + 275 b) 583 + 419.

activities.title: Dice Addition Challenge
activities.materials: paper, a pencil, and three dice (or number cards 0–9).
steps: Roll the three dice to make a 3-digit number (for example 4, 2, 6 becomes 426).
Roll again to make a second 3-digit number.
Estimate the sum by rounding each number to the nearest hundred.
Calculate the exact sum using column addition with carrying.
Compare your estimate with the exact answer. Were they close? Repeat five times.

activities.title: Subtraction Puzzles
steps: Choose a 3-digit number, for example 500.
Create five different subtraction problems that all give the same answer, such as 300.
Check each one by adding your answer back to the number you took away.
Challenge a family member to solve your puzzles.
Make it harder: include at least one subtraction that needs borrowing across a zero.

activities.title: Market Shopping Game
activities.materials: paper, a pencil, some household items, and pretend shilling notes (Sh 5, Sh 10, Sh 20).
steps: Set up a store using items from around your home.
Write a price under Sh 10 on each item, using shillings and cents (for example Sh 3.50).
Take turns being the customer and the shopkeeper.
The customer buys two or three items; the shopkeeper adds up the total and gives the correct change.
Make it harder: write a full receipt showing the items, prices, total, amount paid and change.

activities.title: Estimation Race (Family Game)
activities.materials: paper, a pencil, and a timer.
steps: One player writes down an addition or subtraction of two 3-digit numbers.
Everyone estimates the answer by rounding, as fast as they can.
The player who estimates closest to the exact answer wins a point.
Then work out the exact answer together to see who was nearest.
This game trains you to round quickly and to trust estimation as a checking tool.

activities.title: The Change Investigation
activities.materials: pretend shilling notes and coins, and a few priced items.
steps: Pick an item and note its price, for example Sh 6.40.
Pay with the next whole note above the price (here, Sh 10.00).
Work out the change two ways: by subtracting, and by counting up from the price to the note.
Do both answers agree? Try five different items and record your results in a table.
Investigate: which method do you find quicker – subtracting or counting up? Does it depend on the numbers?

activities.title: Iftar Budget Planner (Family Task)
activities.materials: paper, a pencil, and a pretend budget of Sh 50.00.
steps: Make a shopping list for an iftar meal: dates, bariis, mango, camel milk and sambusa.
Give each item a price in shillings and cents.
Add up the total cost of your list.
Subtract the total from your Sh 50.00 budget to see how much is left. Can you afford one more treat?

After your activities, talk or write about: Which activity helped you understand regrouping best? What was the trickiest part – carrying, borrowing, or lining up the decimal point? What would you like more practice with? Share your answer with your teacher at your next session.

rules.title: Column Method Rule
rules.text: Line up the digits: ones under ones, tens under tens, hundreds under hundreds. Always work from right to left.

rules.title: Carrying Rule
rules.text: (Addition) If a column adds to 10 or more, write the ones digit and carry the ten to the next column. Remember to add the carried digit into the next column.

rules.title: Borrowing Rule
rules.text: (Subtraction) If the top digit is smaller, borrow one from the next column to make ten more. The digit you borrowed from drops by one. To cross a zero, borrow from further along.

rules.title: Money Rule
rules.text: One shilling is 100 cents. Always write two cents digits, e.g. Sh 3.50. Line up the decimal points before adding or subtracting.

terms: Trade ten of one place for one of the next
A rough answer found by rounding first
Change a number to the nearest 10 or 100
Money returned after paying too much
The opposite operation, used to check
commonMistakes: Write the carry above the next column and add it in
Drop that digit by one every time you borrow
If the top is smaller, borrow – do not flip
Borrow from the next column that has something
Put the decimal points in a straight line

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain the key idea in Understanding Money Notation.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Now we bring these skills to money. In this unit we work in shillings, and we use a decimal point to separate whole shillings from cents. There are 100 cents in one shilling, just as there are 100 ones in a group of hundreds – so money is really just place value wearing new clothes. 4 shillings and 24 cents is written Sh 4.24.

questions.question: Calculate 583 + 19.
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, using the Unit 3 methods.
questions.explanation: Ones: 3 + 9 = 12, write 2, carry 1. Tens: 8 + 1 + 1 (carried) = 10, write 0, carry 1. Hundreds: 5 + 1 (carried) = 6. The answer is 602. Two carries in one sum – handled calmly, one column at a time.

questions.question: Calculate 725 + 275.
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, using the Unit 3 methods.
questions.explanation: Ones: 5 + 5 = 10, write 0, carry 1. Tens: 2 + 7 + 1 = 10, write 0, carry 1. Hundreds: 7 + 2 + 1 = 10, write 0, carry 1 into a new thousands place. The answer is 1,000.

questions.question: Calculate 632 − 457.
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, using the Unit 3 methods.
questions.explanation: Ones: 2 − 7 needs a borrow → 12 − 7 = 5 (tens drop from 3 to 2). Tens: 2 − 5 needs a borrow → 12 − 5 = 7 (hundreds drop from 6 to 5). Hundreds: 5 − 4 = 1. The answer is 175. Check: 175 + 457 = 632. Correct!

questions.question: Calculate 800 − 356.
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, using the Unit 3 methods.
questions.explanation: Borrow across the zeros. 800 becomes 7 hundreds, 9 tens and 10 ones. Ones: 10 − 6 = 4. Tens: 9 − 5 = 4. Hundreds: 7 − 3 = 4. The answer is 444.

questions.question: Estimate and then calculate 489 + 325.
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, using the Unit 3 methods.
questions.explanation: Estimate: 490 + 330 = 820. Exact: ones 9 + 5 = 14, write 4 carry 1; tens 8 + 2 + 1 = 11, write 1 carry 1; hundreds 4 + 3 + 1 = 8. The answer is 814, close to the estimate of 820. It checks out.

rounds.explanation: Ones: 5 + 5 = 10, write 0, carry 1. Tens: 2 + 7 + 1 = 10, write 0, carry 1. Hundreds: 7 + 2 + 1 = 10, write 0, carry 1 into a new thousands place. The answer is 1,000.

games.title: Model Detective: Regroup / Carry / Borrow
games.description: Practise regroup / carry / borrow through four short challenges.

rounds.clue: Work it out step by step, using the Unit 3 methods.
rounds.explanation: Ones: 2 − 7 needs a borrow → 12 − 7 = 5 (tens drop from 3 to 2). Tens: 2 − 5 needs a borrow → 12 − 5 = 7 (hundreds drop from 6 to 5). Hundreds: 5 − 4 = 1. The answer is 175. Check: 175 + 457 = 632. Correct!

selfAssessment: I can this unit is built for self-paced learning. That means you move at your own speed. Read each explanation slowly, copy every worked example onto paper and try it yourself, and only move on when you feel ready. Your AI tutor is here day and night to give you extra questions, explain a step a different way, or mark your work. Your teacher will also meet with you about twice a week to help with anything that feels tricky.

I can keep a pencil and paper beside you the whole time. Mathematics is not something you can learn only by reading – you must do it. Line up your digits neatly, work from right to left, and say each step out loud. When you get something wrong, that is not failure; it is a signpost showing you exactly what to practise next. Let us begin.
I can add a 3-digit number to a single-digit, 2-digit or another 3-digit number.
I can subtract a single-digit, 2-digit or 3-digit number from a 3-digit number.
I can regroup correctly when carrying in addition and borrowing in subtraction.
I can subtract across zeros (for example 400 − 256) without getting stuck.
I can estimate sums and differences by rounding to the nearest 10 or 100.
I can write amounts of money in decimal notation (shillings and cents).
